title: ESP Outbound Bytes Spike versus Rolling Baseline

compliance.assurance_rationale: Uplifted from contributing to partial: UC provides meaningful monitoring evidence for CIP-005-6 R2 Part 2.1.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that NERC CIP CIP-005-6 R2 Part 2.1 is enforced — Splunk UC-22.13.16: ESP Outbound Bytes Spike versus Rolling Baseline.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.13.16' running on index=network sourcetype=pan:traffic (bytes_out, src, dest, src_zone), archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

app: Splunk Add-on for Palo Alto Networks (2757)
spl:
index=network sourcetype=pan:traffic src_zone="ESP_INTERNAL" action=allowed earliest=-30d
| eval internal_dest=cidrmatch("10.0.0.0/8",dest) OR cidrmatch("172.16.0.0/12",dest) OR cidrmatch("192.168.0.0/16",dest)
| where internal_dest=0
| bin _time span=1d
| stats sum(bytes_out) as b_out by src, dest, _time
| eventstats median(b_out) as med by src, dest
| eval spike=if(b_out>med*8 AND med>0,1,0)
| where spike=1
| table _time, src, dest, b_out, med
description: We surface unusual outbound volume from ESP-internal segments that may indicate data exfiltration or misconfigured services.
value: Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for NERC CIP compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence using indexes network and sourcetypes pan:traffic, pan:traffic`. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.
implementation: (1) Tune multiplier and internal CIDRs. (2) Exclude CDN and update destinations via lookup. (3) Tag BES Cyber Assets on `src`. (4) Pair with PCAP workflow if available. Indexes required: `index=network`; Sourcetypes: `sourcetype=pan:traffic`, `sourcetype=pan:traffic``.
visualization: Time chart (bytes), Table (spikes), Single value (spike count)
cimSpl: | tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Network_Traffic.All_Traffic by All_Traffic.action, All_Traffic.src, All_Traffic.dest, All_Traffic.dest_port | sort - count

references.title: Splunk Add-on for Palo Alto Networks

knownFalsePositives: ESP access from authorized engineering activities, vendor remote support windows, or scheduled audit observations.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: Splunk Add-on for Palo Alto Networks (2757).
• Ensure the following data sources are available: `index=network` `sourcetype=pan:traffic` (bytes_out, src, dest, src_zone).
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
(1) Tune multiplier and internal CIDRs. (2) Exclude CDN and update destinations via lookup. (3) Tag BES Cyber Assets on `src`. (4) Pair with PCAP workflow if available.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=network sourcetype=pan:traffic src_zone="ESP_INTERNAL" action=allowed earliest=-30d
| eval internal_dest=cidrmatch("10.0.0.0/8",dest) OR cidrmatch("172.16.0.0/12",dest) OR cidrmatch("192.168.0.0/16",dest)
| where internal_dest=0
| bin _time span=1d
| stats sum(bytes_out) as b_out by src, dest, _time
| eventstats median(b_out) as med by src, dest
| eval spike=if(b_out>med*8 AND med>0,1,0)
| where spike=1
| table _time, src, dest, b_out, med
```

Understanding this SPL

**ESP Outbound Bytes Spike versus Rolling Baseline (CIP-005-6 R2 Part 2.1)** — We surface unusual outbound volume from ESP-internal segments that may indicate data exfiltration or misconfigured services.

Documented **Data sources**: `index=network` `sourcetype=pan:traffic` (bytes_out, src, dest, src_zone). **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk Add-on for Palo Alto Networks (2757). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: network; **sourcetype**: pan:traffic. That sourcetype matches what this use case lists under Data sources.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=network, sourcetype=pan:traffic, time bounds. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **internal_dest** — applying business logic to classify, threshold, or derive compliance-relevant indicators.
• Filters the current rows with `where internal_dest=0` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
• Discretizes time or numeric ranges with `bin`/`bucket`.
• `stats` aggregates events by **src, dest, _time**, producing one row per unique combination for trend analysis and threshold evaluation.
• `eventstats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by src, dest** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **spike** — applying business logic to classify, threshold, or derive compliance-relevant indicators.
• Filters the current rows with `where spike=1` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.

Optional CIM / accelerated variant (same use case, normalized fields via Common Information Model):

```spl
| tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Network_Traffic.All_Traffic by All_Traffic.action, All_Traffic.src, All_Traffic.dest, All_Traffic.dest_port | sort - count```

Understanding this CIM / accelerated SPL

**ESP Outbound Bytes Spike versus Rolling Baseline (CIP-005-6 R2 Part 2.1)** — We surface unusual outbound volume from ESP-internal segments that may indicate data exfiltration or misconfigured services.

Documented **Data sources**: `index=network` `sourcetype=pan:traffic` (bytes_out, src, dest, src_zone). **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk Add-on for Palo Alto Networks (2757). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

This **CIM or accelerated** block uses normalized field names and/or `tstats` over data models. Enable **acceleration** on the referenced models (and correct CIM knowledge objects) or the search may return nothing.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Uses `tstats` against accelerated summaries for data model `Network_Traffic.All_Traffic` — enable acceleration for that model.
• Orders rows with `sort` — combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.

Enable Data Model Acceleration (and metric indexes for `mstats`) for the models or datasets referenced above; otherwise `tstats`/`mstats` may return no results from summaries.

Output fields: _time, b_out, dest, internal_dest, med, spike, src.

Step 3 — Validate
Compare with the ESP or perimeter firewall log (for example Palo Alto or FortiGate) for the same window. Spot-check a few source events, reconcile counts, and verify field extractions, thresholds, and index permissions for the people who need this evidence.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Time chart (bytes), Table (spikes), Single value (spike count)
grandmaExplanation: We surface unusual outbound volume from ESP-internal segments that may indicate data exfiltration or misconfigured services. That is important for the big power grid, where proof needs to line up with what the rules expect.
dataModelAcceleration: Enable acceleration for the Network_Traffic data model (Network_Traffic.All_Traffic).
